evidence: goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go defines protocol action tokens (e.g., ActionC2CConnect) and binding IDs, while goseg/system/wifi_c2c_flow_test.go also relies on a separate actions action model and explicitly checks they stay aligned.

goseg/system/wifi_c2c_flow_test.go imports both groundseg/protocol/actions and groundseg/protocol/contracts and contains TestC2CPayloadAndContractsStayAligned, indicating two protocol representations must be manually synchronized.

goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go and goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go each keep local hardcoded validation maps/sets, reinforcing distributed contract authority instead of one authoritative protocol definition.

Large callback-bag runtime structs are repeated across subsystems: `RuntimeContainerOps`/`RuntimeSnapshotOps`/`RuntimeUrbitOps` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`, `StartupRuntime` + `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`, `importerRuntime` in `goseg/importer/importer.go`, and `workflowRuntime` in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`.

Many functions pay repeated resolve/validate indirection before doing work (e.g., `resolveImporterRuntimeFor*` path in `goseg/importer/importer.go`, `resolveWorkflowRuntime` calls in multiple `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` entry points), indicating abstraction overhead at call-sites.
`StartConfEventLoop` and `StartConfEventLoopWithRuntime` in `goseg/config/config_events.go` duplicate nearly the same goroutine/select loop, showing seam indirection without consolidation.
`goseg/system/wifi_c2c_flow_test.go` validates only one protocol contract alignment path (`TestC2CPayloadAndContractsStayAligned`, lines 201-227), focused on C2C connect.
No tests in the scoped test file assert upload contract IDs/actions defined in `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` (lines 11-13, 26-48).

No tests in the scoped test file assert StarTram contract ID/message invariants from `goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go` (lines 12-18, 20-27), despite governance-focused documentation in `README.md` (lines 84-88).

Configuration domain concepts are represented in multiple parallel shapes: `config` package settings snapshots (`goseg/config/config.go`), persisted system config structs (`goseg/structs/configs.go`), and patch DTOs (`goseg/config/config_update_schema.go`) with overlapping fields and naming variants.

The access point runtime type mixes immutable config, mutable runtime defaults, and executable seams in one struct (`goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint.go`), then adds multiple normalization/view wrappers that increase coordination surface.

Urbit transition command handling has repeated step-builder structure with per-command imperative logic (`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go`), while command-to-transition mapping is maintained separately as string maps.

Contract/action invariants are asserted across several modules and tests (`goseg/protocol/actions/actions_test.go`, `goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts_test.go`, `goseg/system/wifi_c2c_flow_test.go`, `goseg/uploadsvc/service_test.go`), indicating a cross-module pattern where one semantic source still requires many local mirrors.

Runtime dependency failures use different taxonomies across modules: plain fmt.Errorf strings in goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go (validateWiFiRuntime, prepareForUse) and aggregated string formatting in goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go (StartupRuntime.validate), while other modules use typed seams.MissingRuntimeDependency errors (e.g. goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go:116-119, goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go:95-100).

Several orchestration/service boundaries return bare errors without operation context (e.g. goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go methods return prepare/runtime errors directly; goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go has many direct return err paths after runtime resolution), while adjacent code in same files wraps with contextual %w messages.

Non-fatal error downgrades are inconsistent and often log-only: optional startup subsystem failures are suppressed to nil in goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:58-60, chunk cleanup failures are only warned and ignored in goseg/importer/importer.go:1228-1231, and wireguard read failures are warned then overwritten in goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go:104-112.

findings.summary: C2C action semantics are owned in both contracts and actions, creating cross-module drift risk.
evidence: protocol_contracts.go declares ActionC2CConnect and contract binding constants as canonical protocol metadata.
wifi_c2c_flow_test.go must verify actions payload values match contracts values in TestC2CPayloadAndContractsStayAligned, proving duplication requires ongoing synchronization checks.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate action token authority into a single module (preferably contract catalog metadata) and have parsing/marshalling consume that source directly or generated code from it; remove parallel hand-maintained action enums.

findings.summary: System WiFi flow tests directly depend on contract registry internals instead of a local boundary.
evidence: wifi_c2c_flow_test.go imports contracts and calls contracts.ActionContractFor(...) inside system tests.
The test couples system behavior checks to protocol catalog internals rather than validating only system-facing behavior, increasing blast radius when contract catalog internals evolve.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a narrow protocol capability interface or exported helper in the actions boundary for system use/tests, so system tests validate behavior without direct dependency on contract catalog lookup mechanics.

findings.summary: Cross-module runtime callback bags create high indirection with low domain cohesion
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` defines many coarse-grained ops structs (`RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, `RuntimeSnapshotOps`, etc.) and these are embedded into higher-level runtimes elsewhere.

`startup_orchestrator.go` carries broad startup callback structs (`startupInitRuntime`, `StartupRuntime`, `startBackgroundServicesRuntime`) with long callback lists, then further wraps them through `resolve*`/`*WithDefaults` paths.

`importer.go` combines storage/upload/provision callbacks plus embedded docker ops into `importerRuntime`, then introduces multiple scoped runtime projections (`initRuntime`, `workflowRuntime`, `publishRuntime`).
`operations_runtime.go` repeats the same pattern via `workflowRuntime` embedding orchestration ops and per-call `resolveWorkflowRuntime`.
`main.go` adds another layer (`bootstrapRuntime`) that forwards into startup orchestration.
findings.suggestion: Replace callback-bag structs with narrower domain services (e.g., `StartupCore`, `ImporterUploadService`, `WorkflowTransitionPublisher`) and inject one composed dependency object per subsystem at construction time. Keep test seams at package boundaries instead of per-function callback fields.

findings.summary: Repeated resolve/validate chains add ceremony and obscure operational flow
evidence: `importer.go` has layered resolvers (`resolveImporterRuntimeFor`, `resolveImporterRuntimeForRequirements`, `resolveImporterRuntimeForInit/Upload/Workflow/Publish`) and many public functions call them before doing core work.

`operations_runtime.go` repeatedly calls `resolveWorkflowRuntime(...)` in separate helpers (`runTransitionLifecycle`, `dispatchUploadImport`, `emitWorkflowTransition`, `publishTransition`, `RunTransitionedOperationWithRuntime`, etc.).

Startup similarly resolves then validates runtime in multiple places (`resolveStartupRuntime`, `resolveStartBackgroundServicesRuntime`, `startupRuntimeWithDefaults`, `startBackgroundServicesRuntimeWithDefaults`).
findings.suggestion: Resolve and validate subsystem runtime once at boundary entry (e.g., request/session/startup phase) and pass a strongly-typed resolved runtime through internal calls. Remove per-helper resolution functions except where a genuinely different scope is required.

findings.summary: Config event loop exposes two near-identical implementations with minimal abstraction gain
evidence: `StartConfEventLoop` and `StartConfEventLoopWithRuntime` both create goroutines with almost identical `select` loops over context cancellation and event channel reads.
The startup default runtime uses `StartConfEventLoop` directly, while a second runtime-aware API exists, leaving two code paths to maintain for the same behavior.
findings.suggestion: Keep one internal loop implementation (runtime-driven) and make `StartConfEventLoop` a thin adapter that delegates to it; then have startup use that single path as well.

findings.summary: Contract governance data is fragmented across constants, specs, and validator maps, creating drift-prone boundaries.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` repeats the same contract facts in const IDs, `protocolContractSpecs`, `expectedByBinding`, and `expectedIDs`, requiring coordinated edits for one contract change.
`startram_contracts.go` repeats the same pattern with separate const/spec/expectedIDs declarations.
README governance references define contract policy locations, but runtime contract declarations are still distributed code-level fragments rather than a canonical catalog artifact.
findings.suggestion: Define one canonical contract catalog structure per family (ID, namespace/action/message/metadata) and generate validator expectations directly from it. Keep manual checks only for invariants not derivable from the schema, and align governance docs to this single runtime source.

findings.summary: Tests exercise only C2C alignment and leave upload and StarTram contract governance paths unverified.
evidence: Only C2C contract alignment is asserted in `TestC2CPayloadAndContractsStayAligned` and `TestC2CActionBindingsCoverKnownActions`.
Upload contract actions (`open-endpoint`, `reset`) and their IDs are defined but not asserted by scoped tests.
StarTram API connection contract message/ID constants are defined but unasserted by scoped tests, despite governance/quality-gate docs emphasizing contract policy.
findings.suggestion: Add a table-driven governance test suite that iterates all exported contract entries across protocol and StarTram families, asserting ID formula stability, required metadata, and compatibility-critical fields (including `APIConnectionErrorMessage`) against expected canonical values.

findings.summary: System configuration semantics are duplicated across runtime snapshots, persistence structs, and patch types, creating a structurally fragmented config model.
evidence: `goseg/config/config.go` defines multiple settings view types (e.g., `StartramSettings`, `AuthSettings`, `RuntimeContext`) that overlap persisted domains.
`goseg/structs/configs.go` defines `SysConfig` and nested domain configs for persistence with similar fields but different composition and naming.

`goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` defines `ConfPatch` and per-domain patch structs with another parallel field map (pointer-based optionality), requiring manual synchronization with both runtime and persistence shapes.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` carries aliases/section constants (`UrbitConfigSection*`) and callback seams dependent on config schema details, widening the blast radius when config domains change.
findings.suggestion: Establish one canonical domain model per config section and derive patch/view representations from it (codegen or thin adapters). Centralize section metadata (field keys, mutability, merge semantics) so update, snapshot, and persistence layers consume shared descriptors instead of hand-maintained parallel structs.

findings.summary: Access point orchestration combines configuration, dependency injection seams, lifecycle control, and process parsing concerns in one runtime surface.
evidence: `AccessPointRuntime` includes both domain config fields (`Wlan`, `SSID`, `Password`, paths) and many behavior seams (`CheckDependenciesFn`, router start/stop, process probing, network interfaces).
The file maintains overlapping adapters (`ConfigView`, `StrategyView`, `resolveAccessPointRuntime`, `normalizeAccessPointRuntime`) that repeatedly transform the same data/behavior bundle.
Lifecycle entry points are duplicated (`StartWithRuntime` and `startWithRuntime`, `StopWithRuntime` and `stopWithRuntime`) with equivalent delegation paths.
Test file size and setup breadth (`goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go`) reflects this mixed surface, requiring many seam-level tests for one module.
findings.suggestion: Split the runtime into explicit `AccessPointConfig` and `AccessPointOps` interfaces, inject ops through a constructor once, and keep lifecycle coordinator APIs on cohesive structs. Remove duplicate start/stop wrappers and consolidate normalization into one construction path.

findings.summary: Urbit transition command handling uses repeated imperative step builders instead of a unified command policy model.
evidence: `urbitTransitionCommandMap` maps command strings to transition types, while execution logic is distributed across many command-specific builders and toggle functions.
`buildUrbitDomainSteps` and `buildMinIODomainSteps` repeat a near-identical sequence: derive alias, validate alias relation, mutate external alias, persist urbit section, and map errors.
Toggle-style functions (`toggleAlias`, `toggleBootStatus`, `toggleAutoReboot`) each directly orchestrate read/decide/persist/publish patterns rather than sharing a policy template.
Tests in `goseg/handler/ship/urbit_test.go` focus heavily on command dispatch/error surfaces, consistent with branching command logic spread across helpers.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a declarative transition command registry where each command defines preconditions, side-effect handlers, and persistence mutations through reusable policy primitives (alias-validated update, toggle-persist-publish, etc.). Keep command strings and behavior definitions in one typed table.

findings.summary: Protocol action/contract invariants are verified in many modules, signaling missing single-source test architecture for shared protocol guarantees.
evidence: `actions_test.go` includes contract compatibility and namespace/action mapping checks.
`contracts_test.go` independently re-verifies binding coverage, ordering, and descriptor linkage.
`wifi_c2c_flow_test.go` adds C2C payload/contract alignment checks (`TestC2CPayloadAndContractsStayAligned`, `TestC2CActionBindingsCoverKnownActions`).
`uploadsvc/service_test.go` reconstructs expected action sets/contracts and dispatch-table parity checks, repeating protocol invariants in service-level tests.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared protocol conformance test suite package that exports reusable assertion helpers and contract fixture matrices; limit module tests to module-specific behavior and consume the shared suite for global protocol invariants.

findings.summary: Equivalent runtime-missing failures are reported with incompatible error forms (typed sentinel vs plain strings), making failure handling unpredictable.
evidence: goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go:42-62 returns plain fmt.Errorf("wifi runtime missing ...") for missing callbacks.
goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:459 returns a concatenated string error for missing callbacks.
goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go:117-119 wraps callback validation with seams.MissingRuntimeDependency("workflow runtime", ...).
goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go:95-100 returns seams.MissingRuntimeDependency for missing runtime callbacks.
findings.suggestion: Standardize runtime dependency failures behind one shared typed error contract (prefer a single helper like seams.MissingRuntimeDependency), then migrate modules to emit that type consistently with contextual wrapping so callers can reliably use errors.Is/errors.As.

findings.summary: Public orchestration/service methods frequently return underlying errors without boundary context, while sibling paths do wrap context.
evidence: goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go has repeated direct `return err` paths in exported methods (e.g. IsWiFiEnabled, RefreshWiFiInfo, ConnectToWiFi, DisconnectWiFi, ToggleDevice, Start/StopWiFiInfoLoop, ApplyCaptiveRules).

goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go similarly returns raw errors after resolveWorkflowRuntime in multiple exported/helper APIs (e.g. lines 230-233, 280-283, 309-312, 319-322, 327-330, 335-338, 385-387).

In contrast, nearby calls in the same modules use contextual wrapping (`resolve wifi device ...: %w`, `prepare workflow runtime ...: %w`), producing inconsistent error detail from similar failure boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Define boundary-level wrapping rules (all exported service/orchestration methods add operation context on return), then apply a focused refactor to wrap bare propagated errors with `%w` and stable operation labels.

findings.summary: Multiple runtime paths downgrade operational errors to warnings and continue, yielding inconsistent and hard-to-predict failure modes.
evidence: goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:58-60 treats optional subsystem init failure as log-only and returns nil.
goseg/importer/importer.go:1228-1231 logs chunk file removal failure and explicitly continues.
goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go:104-112 logs non-ENOENT read failure and proceeds to overwrite config.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit downgrade policy primitives (e.g., non-critical error collector + returned summary or health signal), so log-and-continue cases are intentional, traceable, and uniformly surfaced instead of silently divergent by module.

findings.summary: Process-wide mutable runtime singletons create hidden cross-module coupling
evidence: click runtime resolves docker behavior through lifecycle.DefaultRuntime() rather than an explicit dependency seam.
docker lifecycle exposes global runtime mutation via SetDefaultRuntime, enabling process-wide behavior changes from unrelated modules.
broadcast loop/state rely on default process-wide runtime/controller instances.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit dependency wiring at subsystem boundaries (startup/bootstrap), pass runtime interfaces via constructors, and keep package-level defaults as thin compatibility shims only.

findings.summary: Several error paths lose causal context by formatting with %v instead of %w
evidence: executeClickCommand and parsing paths use fmt.Errorf(... %v, err), preventing errors.Is/errors.As traversal.
llama orchestration uses mixed wrapped/unwrapped formatting for runtime failures.
docker lifecycle mixes casing and style conventions in error strings, reducing predictability for operators and tests.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on wrapped errors (%w) for propagated failures, reserve plain strings for terminal boundaries, and enforce one error-message style guide (lowercase, action/context first) across lifecycle/click/orchestration packages.

findings.summary: Config storage path uses stdout fallbacks instead of structured error handling
evidence: storage.go prints failures/fallback decisions with fmt.Println/fmt.Printf and proceeds silently.
Sibling config modules generally return wrapped errors, so caller-level handling is inconsistent.
findings.suggestion: Refactor storage path resolution to return typed/wrapped errors (or explicit warning values) and route user-visible diagnostics through the project logger at call boundaries.

findings.summary: Repeated runtime interfaces/wrappers add indirection with little behavioral leverage
evidence: Multiple runtime interfaces are thin pass-throughs to one implementation and mostly mirror function signatures.
Each package reimplements similar SetRuntime/reset/global runtime patterns.
findings.suggestion: Collapse repetitive per-package runtime scaffolding into one shared execution seam (or explicit service object) and keep wrappers only where they add policy, validation, or translation.

findings.summary: Large wrapper hubs repeat validation/error policy across many near-identical functions
evidence: click.go contains many similar methods with duplicated argument validation and error wrapping patterns.
config_update_registry.go centralizes a very large patch/merge matrix in one file, increasing cognitive load and making policy edits broad.
findings.suggestion: Use a small command descriptor table + shared validator/wrapper helper for click operations, and split config patch registry by domain section (connectivity/auth/penpai/etc.) with composable registration.
